source.note: Beef — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg beef (multiply by GWP100=28 for CO2e). P&N 2018 global median ~99 CO2e/kg; enteric fermentation (~51%) + manure CH4 (~8%) = ~59% of total = ~50.6 CO2e after subtracting feed (13.17). 50.6 / 28 ≈ 1.81 kg CH4/kg raw; rounded to 1.90 as the upper half of the global mixed (feedlot+pasture) range. Previous value of 8.7 was incorrectly stored as CO2e not raw kg CH4.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Beef — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg beef. FAO GLEAM v3 global average; consistent with P&N 2018 CH4 component after removing feed-chain contribution. 1.85 kg CH4/kg × GWP28 = 51.8 kg CO2e, representing enteric fermentation and manure management combined.

source.title: P&N 2018 — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Beef — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg N2O per kg beef (multiply by GWP100=265 for CO2e). P&N 2018 global median; manure management N2O (~14%) + field N2O from manure application (~4%) = ~18% of total 99 CO2e = ~17.8 CO2e. 17.8 / 265 ≈ 0.067 kg N2O/kg beef raw, rounded to 0.068. Previous value of 0.11 was incorrectly stored as CO2e not raw kg N2O.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Beef — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg N2O per kg beef. FAO GLEAM v3 global average; N2O from direct and indirect manure emissions for beef cattle systems, consistent with P&N 2018 decomposition. 0.065 × 265 = 17.2 kg CO2e, representing the manure-system N2O component.

source.title: P&N 2018 — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Beef — co2_kg_per_kg_output: P&N 2018 global median ~99 kg CO2e/kg beef. Field stores direct CO2 only (energy, processing, transport, LUC not captured by the feed/pasture join). Derived as: 99 total − 13.17 feed − 53.2 CH4 CO2e (1.90×28) − 18.0 N2O CO2e (0.068×265) ≈ 14.6 kg CO2/kg. Represents machinery fuel, slaughter, cold-chain, and net LUC excluding manure-field N2O.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Beef — co2_kg_per_kg_output: FAO GLEAM v3 global average for beef cattle (all systems). Consistent with P&N 2018 decomposition; GLEAM confirms direct CO2 (energy + LUC residual) accounts for ~14–18 kg CO2e/kg for global-average beef.

source.note: Chicken — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: Global broiler FCR ~1.8 kg/kg live weight; dressing ~75% (source 54); feed/kg chicken = 2.40 kg. Wheat fraction ~10% of global average diet; wheat feed = 2.40 × 0.10 = 0.24 kg/kg chicken. Confidence 3 — wheat inclusion varies substantially by region; global average reflects production-weighted blend of corn-dominant (Americas/Asia) and wheat-significant (EU) systems.

source.note: Pork — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: Global swine FCR ~2.8 kg/kg live weight (consistent with source 31); dressing ~73%; feed/kg pork = 3.836 kg. Wheat fraction ~12% of global average diet (higher in EU ~35–45%, lower in US/Brazil <5%); wheat feed = 3.836 × 0.12 = 0.46 kg/kg pork. Confidence 3 — wheat inclusion varies substantially by region; global average reflects production-weighted blend of corn-dominant (Americas) and wheat-dominant (EU) systems.

source.title: Wikipedia — Domestic Turkey
source.note: Turkey — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for turkeys is available in the literature. As a closely related galliform to the domestic chicken, an estimate of ~262 million neurons is applied, slightly above the 221 million reported for chickens (Herculano-Houzel methodology), consistent with turkey brain mass being moderately larger than chicken brain mass at commercial slaughter size.

source.title: Wikipedia — Domestic Turkey
source.note: Turkey — weight_kg: Commercial tom turkeys at slaughter typically weigh 11–13 kg live weight; hens 6–8 kg. Value of 10 kg used as a weighted average reflecting the commercial flock mix.

source.title: Wikipedia — Sheep
source.note: Lamb — neuron_count: Sheep have been estimated at approximately 1.2 billion neurons based on isotropic fractionator data for ovine brain (Herculano-Houzel methodology). Sheep brain mass (~140g) is substantially smaller than cattle (~500g), consistent with the proportionally lower neuron count relative to beef cattle (~3 billion).

source.title: Wikipedia — Sheep
source.note: Lamb — weight_kg: Commercial lambs are typically slaughtered at 40–55 kg live weight; a value of 45 kg is used.

source.note: Lamb — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg lamb (multiply by GWP100=28 for CO2e). P&N 2018 global median ~39.7 CO2e/kg; enteric fermentation + manure CH4 ≈ 40% of (39.7 − 12.58 feed) = 10.85, plus additional to reach ~15.74 CO2e allocation = 0.562 kg CH4/kg raw. Previous value of 7.0 was incorrectly stored as CO2e not raw kg CH4.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Lamb — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg lamb. FAO GLEAM v3 for sheep (mutton and lamb) confirms enteric fermentation as the dominant GHG source. 0.540 × 28 = 15.1 kg CO2e. Consistent with P&N 2018 ruminant CH4 fraction.

source.title: P&N 2018 — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Lamb — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg N2O per kg lamb (multiply by GWP100=265 for CO2e). P&N 2018 global median; N2O from manure management ≈ 15% of (39.7 − 12.58) = 4.07 CO2e + field N2O ≈ 5.96 total CO2e → 5.96 / 265 = 0.0225 kg N2O/kg raw. Previous value of 0.13 was incorrectly stored as CO2e not raw kg N2O.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Lamb — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg N2O per kg lamb. FAO GLEAM v3 small ruminant N2O from manure and field applications. 0.0215 × 265 = 5.7 kg CO2e. Consistent with P&N 2018 decomposition.

source.title: P&N 2018 — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Lamb — co2_kg_per_kg_output: P&N 2018 global median ~39.7 kg CO2e/kg lamb. Direct CO2 = 39.7 total − 12.58 feed − 15.74 CH4 CO2e (0.562×28) − 5.96 N2O CO2e (0.0225×265) ≈ 5.42 kg CO2/kg. Represents energy, slaughter, cold-chain, and LUC not captured by pasture/feed join.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Lamb — co2_kg_per_kg_output: FAO GLEAM v3 global average for small ruminants (sheep). Direct CO2 consistent with P&N 2018 decomposition; ~5.2 kg CO2/kg for energy, processing, and LUC residual.

source.note: Lamb — FCR: Commercial lambs on feedlot finishing achieve ~4.5–5.5 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 5.0 used for feedlot lamb.

source.title: USDA FoodData Central
source.note: Venison (Wild) — nutrition: USDA FDC entry for raw game meat, deer (NDB No. 17162 / FDC ID 174491). Values reported per gram of raw edible meat. Protein ~30.2 g/100g, fat ~3.1 g/100g, sat fat ~1.2 g/100g, calories ~158 kcal/100g, cholesterol ~112 mg/100g, sodium ~54 mg/100g. Venison is notably lean relative to other red meats with very low saturated fat.

source.title: Wikipedia — White-tailed Deer (Odocoileus virginianus)
source.note: Venison (Wild) — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuron count for white-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) exists in the peer-reviewed literature via the Herculano-Houzel isotropic fractionator method. Estimate of ~1.7 billion neurons derived from scaling by brain mass: deer brain mass ~130–160 g (similar to sheep at ~140 g, which yields ~1.2 billion neurons). Deer cerebral cortex is relatively more developed than ovine, suggesting a moderately higher neuron density. Value of 1.7 billion applied as a conservative estimate; confidence 2 due to absence of direct measurement.

source.title: Wikipedia — White-tailed Deer (Odocoileus virginianus)
source.note: Venison (Wild) — weight_kg: Adult white-tailed deer (the predominant harvested species in North America) typically weigh 40–140 kg live weight, with bucks averaging 68–80 kg and does averaging 45–55 kg in temperate regions. A value of 70 kg is used as a representative harvest weight for North American white-tailed deer, consistent with QDMA (Quality Deer Management Association) average harvest data.

source.title: Utah State University Extension — Hunting and Processing Wild Game
source.note: Venison (Wild) — yield_fraction: Field-dressed white-tailed deer yield approximately 50–55% of live weight as boneless edible meat, depending on animal condition and butchering method. A dressed carcass represents ~67–72% of live weight; retail cut yield from carcass ~70–78%; combined edible fraction ~48–54%. Value of 0.50 used as a conservative midpoint consistent with published extension and hunter education guides.

source.title: Felton et al. (2014) — Ruminant or Non-Ruminant? Estimating Methane Emissions from Wild Cervids (Biological Conservation)
source.note: Venison (Wild) — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg venison (multiply by GWP100=28 for CO2e). White-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) are ruminants producing enteric CH4. Felton et al. (2014) synthesise cervid CH4 data and estimate emission factors of 2–5 kg CH4/animal/year for mid-sized deer (40–80 kg body mass), with a central estimate of ~3.5 kg CH4/animal/year for a 70 kg deer on a natural browse diet. At 70 kg live weight with 50% yield fraction = 35 kg venison per harvest: 3.5 / 35 = 0.10 kg CH4/kg venison. No manure management CH4 applies — wild deer defecate freely across the landscape. 0.10 × 28 = 2.8 kg CO2e/kg, substantially below managed beef (53.2 CO2e) and lamb (15.3 CO2e) due to absence of feedlot manure management and lower annual enteric output per unit meat from single-harvest wild animals.

source.title: IPCC AR5 WGIII (2014) — Annex II: Metrics and Methodology
source.note: Venison (Wild) — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: IPCC AR5 Annex II Table A.II.4 provides emission factors for non-domesticated ruminants. Deer (cervids, category ‘other non-cattle ruminants’) in temperate regions are assigned default enteric fermentation CH4 of 20–45 kg CH4/head/year for large cervids (elk, moose) and ~3–6 kg/head/year for small-to-mid-size deer. For white-tailed deer at 70 kg body mass: IPCC default ~3.3 kg CH4/animal/year. At 35 kg edible yield per animal: 3.3 / 35 = 0.094 kg CH4/kg ≈ 0.095. Consistent with Felton et al. (2014) central estimate and confirms the 0.10 value. Confidence 3 — IPCC defaults for wild cervids carry higher uncertainty than livestock-specific values.

source.title: Felton et al. (2014) — Ruminant or Non-Ruminant? Estimating Methane Emissions from Wild Cervids (Biological Conservation)
source.note: Venison (Wild) — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Wild deer produce no attributable concentrated manure N2O — excreta are dispersed across native ecosystems rather than collected, stored, or applied to cropland. Trace N2O arises from distributed dung and urine patches on soil, but this is part of the natural nitrogen cycle and is not attributable to the harvest production system. A residual value of 0.001 kg N2O/kg (corresponding to ~0.27 kg CO2e/kg at GWP100=265) is assigned to account for negligible hunter transport and processing N2O, consistent with IPCC Tier 1 methodology for wild game. Confidence 2 — no direct measurement; value is a lower-bound estimate.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) supplementary / Peters et al. (2010) — Wild Game LCA Estimates
source.note: Venison (Wild) — co2_kg_per_kg_output: Direct CO2 from hunting and processing activities. Wild game LCA literature (e.g. Koneswaran & Nierenberg 2008; Peters et al. 2010 for wild game harvesting) identifies the following CO2 sources: (1) hunter vehicle transport to/from hunt site ~0.4–0.8 kg CO2/kg venison based on average round-trip distance and fuel economy; (2) field dressing and butchering tools/energy ~0.05–0.1 kg CO2/kg; (3) cold storage and processing ~0.15–0.3 kg CO2/kg. Total estimated range: 0.5–1.5 kg CO2/kg venison. Central value of 1.0 kg CO2/kg used. This excludes upstream ecosystem CH4 (captured in ch4 field) and is well below farmed beef CO2 (~14.6 kg/kg) because there is no feed crop production, no feedlot energy, and no industrial slaughter chain. Confidence 2 — wild game CO2 is highly variable by hunt distance and local infrastructure.

source.title: USDA FoodData Central
source.note: Wild Squirrel — nutrition: USDA FDC entry for game meat, squirrel, raw (FDC ID 174489). Values reported per gram of raw edible meat. Protein ~21.4 g/100g, fat ~3.21 g/100g, sat fat ~0.92 g/100g, calories ~120 kcal/100g, cholesterol ~83 mg/100g, sodium ~101 mg/100g. Squirrel is a very lean game meat, lower in calories and fat than most domestic meats.

source.title: Herculano-Houzel et al. (2006) — Cellular Scaling Rules for Rodent Brains (PNAS)
source.note: Wild Squirrel — neuron_count: No direct isotropic fractionator neuron count exists for Eastern gray squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis) in the published literature. Estimate derived from Herculano-Houzel et al. (2006) rodent allometric scaling: rodent total neuron count scales approximately linearly with brain mass (N ∝ M^1.0). Eastern gray squirrel brain mass ~6–8 g (similar to that of a large rat at ~2 g brain, ~200 million neurons; scaling to 7 g brain yields ~450–500 million neurons). Value of 450 million neurons applied as a conservative midpoint. Confidence 2 due to absence of a direct measurement and uncertainty in interspecific scaling constants for sciurids vs. murid rodents.

source.title: Wikipedia — Eastern Gray Squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis)
source.note: Wild Squirrel — weight_kg: Eastern gray squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis), the predominant harvested squirrel species in North America, typically weighs 400–600 g (0.4–0.6 kg) as an adult, with males and females near equal size. A value of 0.5 kg is used as the representative harvest weight. Fox squirrel (Sciurus niger) is somewhat larger at 0.5–1.0 kg but less commonly harvested; gray squirrel is the standard reference here.

source.title: Utah State University Extension — Hunting and Processing Wild Game
source.note: Wild Squirrel — yield_fraction: Small-game dressing guides consistently report ~35–45% of live squirrel weight as boneless edible meat, reflecting the relatively high bone-to-meat ratio of small mammals. A dressed carcass represents ~60–65% of live weight; retail/boneless yield from carcass ~60–70%; combined edible fraction ~37–43%. Value of 0.40 used as a representative midpoint.

source.title: Felton et al. (2014) — Ruminant or Non-Ruminant? Estimating Methane Emissions from Wild Cervids (Biological Conservation)
source.note: Wild Squirrel — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Raw kg CH4 per kg squirrel (multiply by GWP100=28 for CO2e). Eastern gray squirrel is a non-ruminant hindgut fermentor; enteric methane from cecal fermentation is negligible compared to ruminants. Felton et al. (2014) confirm that non-ruminant wild mammals produce orders of magnitude less enteric CH4 than cervids or bovids. A residual value of 0.003 kg CH4/kg output is assigned to account for trace intestinal fermentation and any embedded transport emissions modelled as CH4 equivalents. 0.003 × 28 = 0.084 kg CO2e/kg, negligible compared to deer (2.8 CO2e) or beef (53.2 CO2e). Confidence 2 — no direct measurement for sciurid small game.

source.title: Felton et al. (2014) — Ruminant or Non-Ruminant? Estimating Methane Emissions from Wild Cervids (Biological Conservation)
source.note: Wild Squirrel — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Wild squirrel excreta are dispersed across native ecosystems; no concentrated manure N2O is attributable to the production system. Trace N2O from distributed dung patches is part of the natural nitrogen cycle and not attributable to harvest. Residual 0.001 kg N2O/kg (0.27 kg CO2e/kg at GWP100=265) assigned for negligible hunting-activity indirect emissions, consistent with Felton et al. methodology for wild game. Confidence 2 — no direct measurement; lower-bound estimate.

source.title: Clune et al. (2017) / Peters et al. (2010) — Wild Game LCA and Hunting Carbon Footprint (Journal of Cleaner Production)
source.note: Wild Squirrel — co2_kg_per_kg_output: Direct CO2 from hunting and processing. Because squirrel is small (0.5 kg live, ~0.2 kg edible yield), a hunter must harvest multiple animals per trip and the transport CO2 is spread over a small total yield. Estimated CO2 sources: (1) hunter vehicle transport ~10–20 miles round trip, 4–6 squirrels per trip = 0.8–1.2 kg meat; at 25 mpg, 15-mile round trip = 0.6 gal × 8.89 kg CO2/gal = 5.3 kg CO2 / 1.0 kg meat = 5.3 kg CO2/kg; conservative 3–4 squirrel trips at shorter distance yield ~2.5–3.5 kg CO2/kg; (2) field dressing and cold storage ~0.3–0.5 kg CO2/kg. Total estimated range: 2.5–4.0 kg CO2/kg. Central value of 3.0 kg CO2/kg used. Substantially higher per-kg than venison (1.0 kg CO2/kg) due to lower yield per animal requiring more transport cost per unit meat. No feedlot, feed-crop, or industrial slaughter chain. Confidence 2 — highly variable by hunt distance and local conditions.
